title: 'No Kings' Protests Draw Massive Crowds Nationwide; Congress Leaves on Recess Without DHS Deal
detail: Hundreds of thousands of Americans participated in 'No Kings' rallies across the country on March 28, 2026, with over 100,000 demonstrators at the Minnesota State Capitol alone. Protesters cited the DHS partial government shutdown, ongoing ICE enforcement operations, and what they characterized as authoritarian overreach by the Trump administration. The day's protests coincided with Congress departing for a two-week recess without resolving the 43-day DHS funding standoff — making it the longest partial government shutdown in U.S. history. ICE, funded separately through the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act,' remained unaffected and fully operational throughout.

media.caption: Massive No Kings protests draw millions nationwide on March 28

title: Protesters Breach Portland ICE Facility Gate; Police Declare Riot After 'No Kings' March
detail: Following the Portland 'No Kings' march at Tom McCall Waterfront Park on March 28, a subset of protesters moved to the ICE field office building in South Portland. Protesters broke through the front gate of the ICE facility. Federal agents pushed them back. Portland Police declared a riot and ordered all demonstrators to disperse. At approximately 9 p.m., 26-year-old Isiah Lee climbed onto the facility's roof and began vandalizing it; he was arrested and charged with first-degree criminal mischief and second-degree criminal trespass. Portland Police made three total arrests for criminal mischief and assault. Protesters burned an American flag and a Nazi flag outside the facility. An appeals court had recently reversed a ruling that had limited federal agents' use of tear gas outside the ICE facility, raising concerns among nearby residents about chemical munitions. Since June 2025, Portland police had made 89 total arrests in connection with ICE-related protest activity.

media.caption: Protesters breach Portland ICE facility gate during No Kings march

title: Senate Passes DHS Funding Bill Excluding ICE/CBP by Voice Vote at 2:20 a.m.
detail: In a highly unusual late-night session, the Senate passed a bill at 2:20 a.m. on March 28 to fund all of DHS except ICE and CBP, approved by voice vote. The bill represented Democrats' final offer before Congress left for a two-week recess. The House had already rejected this approach the previous day. Senate Democrats contended that ICE needed significant reforms before receiving new funding, citing the DHS shutdown they had triggered on February 14 following the fatal shooting of Minneapolis nurse Alex Pretti by DHS law enforcement. Trump publicly stated: 'You can't have a bill that's not going to fund ICE.' With no deal reached, the shutdown became the longest partial government shutdown in U.S. history.
